title: Travis Kalanick raised $1.7B for his robotics comeback, and Uber chipped in
excerpt: Travis Kalanick is back, and Uber is helping to fund him. The founder Uber forced out in 2017 has raised $1.7 billion for Atoms, an industrial-AI and robotics company he has built in near-total stealth for years. Andreessen Horowitz led the round. Its co-founder Ben Horowitz is joining the board. And among the investors sits […]
content_html:
Travis Kalanick is back, and Uber is helping to fund him. The founder Uber forced out in 2017 has raised $1.7 billion for Atoms, an industrial-AI and robotics company he has built in near-total stealth for years.

Andreessen Horowitz led the round. Its co-founder Ben Horowitz is joining the board. And among the investors sits Uber, the company Kalanick started in 2009 and left under a cloud.

That exit still shadows the story. Uber pushed Kalanick out as chief executive in 2017 after complaints of sexual harassment, discrimination and a toxic workplace. Nine years on, his old company is writing him a cheque. Kalanick, never one for understatement, calls the round “unfinished business”.

The equity comes with serious debt. Alongside a16z, Bain Capital, Fifth Wall and others, Atoms lined up credit facilities from JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, Wells Fargo and Barclays. The company did not disclose a valuation. That is the kind of firepower needed to build heavy machines, not apps.

Bits to atoms

Kalanick’s pitch is a single idea he has chased for 16 years: turning the physical world into something software can run. In his framing, manufacturing is the processor, real estate is the storage, and transport is the network. Uber digitised transport for the masses.

CloudKitchens, his ghost-kitchen venture, did the same for food. Atoms is meant to do it for whole industrial sectors. He calls the result an “atoms-based computer.”

The target is the unglamorous heart of the economy: mining, construction, heavy transport and food. His term for the toolkit is Industrial AI, a mix of software, sensors, robotics and models aimed at automating entire sectors. He calls the wider shift the “Age of Atoms.”

Not a humanoid in sight

Here Kalanick parts ways with much of the field. While rivals pour billions into general-purpose humanoid robots, Atoms builds specialised machines for specific jobs. Horowitz argues that purpose-built hardware copes with brutal industrial environments far better than a humanoid could. It is a pointed bet against the hottest trend in physical AI.

Atoms is split into three parts, according to reports. Atoms Food folds in CloudKitchens and its cooking and delivery software. Atoms Mining puts autonomous machines to work at extraction sites, built on Atoms’ acquisition of Pronto, a startup run by former Uber and Google engineer Anthony Levandowski.

Atoms Transport is what Kalanick calls a “wheelbase for robots.”

Levandowski’s presence adds intrigue. He sat at the centre of a self-driving trade-secrets case involving Google and Uber, and later received a presidential pardon. Kalanick has also flirted with buying the US arm of China’s Pony AI, with Uber’s help, though those talks ended earlier this year.

Comeback, with caveats

The mood around the deal is euphoric. One a16z partner called it the largest cheque the firm has ever written. Another investor predicted Atoms would be worth a trillion dollars within a decade. Horowitz put it more simply: “Travis is back.”

Some scepticism is warranted. Atoms has revealed a grand vision and a very large bank balance, but little in the way of deployed industrial robots at scale. The valuation is a mystery, the claims are sweeping, and Kalanick’s Uber record is not easily forgotten. Yet the bet is coherent.

He turned the movement of people into a software business once. Now, with $1.7 billion and his old adversary alongside him, he wants to do the same to the machines that grow, dig and haul the physical world.

sentences.en: He turned the movement of people into a software business once.
sentences.zh: 他曾经把人员流动变成了软件业务。

sentences.en: Now, with $1.7 billion and his old adversary alongside him, he wants to do the same to the machines that grow, dig and haul the physical world.
sentences.zh: 现在，有了 17 亿美元和他的老对手的支持，他希望对那些在物理世界中种植、挖掘和运输的机器做同样的事情。

content_html_zh:
特拉维斯·卡兰尼克回来了，优步正在帮助资助他。 2017 年被赶出 Uber 的创始人已为 Atoms 筹集了 17 亿美元，这是一家他多年来几乎完全秘密创建的工业人工智能和机器人公司。

安德森·霍洛维茨 (Andreessen Horowitz) 领投本轮融资。 其联合创始人本·霍洛维茨 (Ben Horowitz) 将加入董事会。 Uber 也是投资者之一，该公司卡兰尼克于 2009 年创立，后来黯然离场。

这个出口仍然给故事蒙上了阴影。 2017 年，由于受到性骚扰、歧视和有毒工作场所的投诉，Uber 罢免了卡兰尼克首席执行官的职务。 九年后，他的老公司给他开了一张支票。 卡兰尼克从不轻描淡写，他称这一轮为“未完成的事业”。

股权伴随着严重的债务。 除了 a16z、贝恩资本、Fifth Wall 等公司之外，Atoms 还获得了摩根大通、高盛、美国银行、富国银行和巴克莱银行的信贷融资。 该公司没有透露估值。 这是建造重型机器所需的火力，而不是应用程序。

比特到原子

卡兰尼克的主张是他追求了 16 年的一个想法：将物理世界变成软件可以运行的东西。 在他的框架中，制造业是处理器，房地产是存储，运输是网络。 优步为大众提供数字化交通。

他的幽灵厨房企业“CloudKitchens”在食品方面也做了同样的事情。 Atoms 旨在为整个工业部门做到这一点。 他将这一成果称为“基于原子的计算机”。

目标是经济的乏味核心：采矿、建筑、重型运输和食品。 他对该工具包的术语是“工业人工智能”，它是软件、传感器、机器人和模型的组合，旨在实现整个行业的自动化。 他将更广泛的转变称为“原子时代”。

看不见人形生物

卡兰尼克在这里与该领域的大部分人分道扬镳。 当竞争对手在通用人形机器人上投入数十亿美元时，Atoms 则为特定工作制造专用机器。 霍洛维茨认为，专用硬件能够比人形机器人更好地应对残酷的工业环境。 这是针对物理人工智能最热门趋势的针对性押注。

据报道，Atoms 分为三个部分。 Atoms Food 包含在 CloudKitchens 及其烹饪和配送软件中。 Atoms Mining 在 Atoms 收购 Pronto（一家由前 Uber 和 Google 工程师 Anthony Levandowski 运营的初创公司）的基础上，将自动化机器投入开采现场工作。

原子传输被卡兰尼克称为“机器人的轴距”。

莱万多夫斯基的出现增添了阴谋。 他是涉及谷歌和优步的自动驾驶商业秘密案的中心人物，后来获得了总统赦免。 卡兰尼克还曾考虑在 Uber 的帮助下收购中国小马智行的美国子公司，不过这些谈判已于今年早些时候结束。

回归，有警告

围绕该交易的气氛十分乐观。 a16z 的一位合伙人称这是该公司有史以来开出的最大的支票。 另一位投资者预测 Atoms 的价值将在十年内达到一万亿美元。 霍洛维茨说得更简单：“特拉维斯回来了。”

一些怀疑是有道理的。 Atoms 展现了宏伟的愿景和庞大的银行余额，但在大规模部署工业机器人方面却鲜有体现。 估值是个谜，索赔声波浩大，而卡兰尼克的 Uber 记录也不容易被遗忘。 然而，这个赌注是连贯的。

他曾经把人员流动变成了软件业务。 现在，有了 17 亿美元和他的老对手的支持，他希望对那些在物理世界中种植、挖掘和运输的机器做同样的事情。
